title: Long-tail cast on
subtitle: The two-strand starter every UK knitting pattern assumes
excerpt: The standard British cast-on: thumb-and-finger slingshot grip, working yarn cast over the needle, tail folded round the new loop. Stretchy enough for ribbing, neat enough for stocking stitch, fast enough that ninety stitches goes on in two minutes. The first move of almost every UK knitting pattern.
sourceNotes: Weldon's Practical Knitting (1880s–1900s, public domain via Internet Archive) — documents the two-strand cast-on as the standard starter, called variously the German cast-on, the Continental cast-on, or the long-tail cast-on across the Victorian and Edwardian English knitting literature. Mary Thomas, Knitting Book (1938, out of copyright in the UK/EU as Thomas died in 1948) — describes the slingshot grip as the studio default for British knitters. The slingshot hand position predates the printed sources by centuries and is the standard worldwide.

content.text: Almost every UK knitting pattern starts with "cast on N stitches" and leaves the method to the knitter. The default that almost every UK pattern assumes is the
long-tail cast on

(US: long-tail cast on — identical). It produces a neat braided edge on the right side of the work and a row of small bumps on the wrong side. The cast-on is the first row of knitting, so the second row of the pattern starts immediately after, no separate setup row.

The trick the long-tail cast on adds to a single-strand cast-on is measuring out a tail long enough to cast on every stitch before starting. Run out of tail halfway and the cast-on has to start over. The reliable rule for DK yarn: 2.5 cm of tail per stitch, plus 15 cm spare for weaving in. So 60 stitches needs about 1.65 m of tail.
What you need
A ball of DK yarn.
Any smooth DK in any fibre. A smooth light-coloured yarn is easier to read while learning; dark fluffy yarns blur the loops.
A 4 mm needle.
Standard DK pairing in UK convention (the US equivalent is size 6). Any needle 4–5 mm is fine for practice.
Soft measuring tape, sharp scissors, tapestry needle.
The measuring tape is for the tail; the tapestry needle is for weaving in once the practice swatch is finished.
The cast-on, step by step
Step 1 — Measure the tail.
Pull yarn from the ball without cutting. Measure 2.5 cm of
tail
per stitch to be cast on, then add 15 cm spare for weaving in. For a learning practice of twenty stitches, measure 65 cm of tail. Hold the measured point — this is where the slip knot will go.
Step 2 — Make a slip knot at the measured point.

Take the measured point of the yarn, fold a loop, and pull a small bight of yarn through the loop to make a slip knot. Place the slip knot on the needle and tighten gently — not so tight the knot can't slide. The slip knot is the first cast-on stitch. The cut tail end now hangs from the slip knot, with the
working yarn
coming from the ball.
Step 3 — Set the slingshot grip.

Hold the needle in the right hand. With the left hand, take both strands of yarn (tail and working yarn) between the palm and the three smallest fingers — clamp them lightly. The tail hangs down off the side of the thumb; the working yarn hangs down off the side of the index finger. Bring the thumb and index finger up so the strands form a wide V. Two horizontal strands — one across the front of the thumb, one across the front of the index finger — and the needle held above the V. This is the
slingshot grip
Step 4 — The cast-on motion.

Bring the tip of the needle down through the loop on the thumb (from the front, under the lower strand of the thumb loop). Carry the needle up and over the working yarn on the index finger (catching it from front to back). Bring the needle back down through the thumb loop. Release the thumb. The new stitch sits on the needle; the tail end has wrapped around the base of the new stitch. The thumb returns to pick up the tail again from below and the slingshot re-forms.
Said slower: under-the-thumb-loop, over-the-index-yarn, back-through-the-thumb-loop, release the thumb. Four moves, one new stitch. Repeated until the count is right.
Step 5 — Check the stitch count.

Count the loops on the needle, including the slip knot. The slip knot is stitch number one. Cast on until the count matches the pattern. For the first practice, aim for twenty stitches — enough to feel the rhythm without being so many that a miscount means starting over.
Step 6 — Begin the pattern.

The cast-on row is row 1 of the pattern. The next row to work is row 2 — start as the pattern says (often a wrong-side row of purls for stocking stitch, or the start of the rib for k1 p1 ribbing). The tail-end is left hanging at the right edge for weaving in once the piece is finished.
How to know it's right

The cast-on edge shows two faces: a row of horizontal Vs on one side (the right side, the side shown on a finished piece) and a row of small bumps on the other (the wrong side, the inside of a hat or the back of a scarf). The stitches lie evenly along the needle, not crammed at the slip knot end and loose at the working end. The edge has the gentle give of a
stretchy edge

— pull the cast-on stitches sideways and they spread without distorting the row above. A perfectly slack cast-on is wrong; a perfectly tight cast-on is wrong; the right tension is the same tension as the rows that follow.
Common mistakes
The tail runs out before the stitch count is reached.

The 2.5 cm-per-stitch rule was understated. For thicker yarns (aran, chunky), the per-stitch tail length grows: 3.5 cm per stitch for aran, 5 cm for chunky. For thinner yarns (4-ply, lace), 2 cm per stitch is enough. Drop the half-cast-on, cut and start again with more tail.
The edge is too tight to knit into on row 2.

The cast-on tension was too tight, or the needle pulling each new stitch onto the needle was angled steeply. Cast on over two needles held together — a common studio fix — to make the loops one needle-width larger; pull one needle out before starting row 2.
The cast-on edge ripples or curls.

Tension was uneven across the cast-on or the working yarn and tail were swapped. Check: when the slingshot is set, the tail is on the thumb side and the working yarn is on the index-finger side. The other way round gives a backwards cast-on that ripples on the right side.
Stitches twist on the needle.

The needle came back through the thumb loop in the wrong direction. The motion is down-through, over-the-index, back-through-the-thumb in one continuous loop — the needle re-traces its path. Reversing the direction of the thumb pull twists the stitch.
Where this cast-on goes next

The long-tail cast on is the default starter for the vast majority of UK knitting patterns. For ribbed cuffs that need more stretch (socks worked cuff-down, beanies, gloves), variants like German twisted or Jenny's stretchy cast-on are worth learning later. For provisional starts (where the cast-on edge will be unzipped and worked in the other direction later), a crochet provisional cast-on is the standard alternative. For now, this is the move every other piece begins with — and the move that, with twenty repeats, settles into muscle memory inside a single learning session.

recipeTools.notes: For weaving in the cast-on tail at the end of the project. Standard tapestry-needle aliases include darning needle and yarn needle.
For measuring the tail length before casting on — see Step 1.
